planting.note: Plant in spring in well-drained soil in full sun to light shade.

directions: Plant this deciduous species in average, freely draining soil where it forms an open, twiggy shrub. It is valued as a long-flowering parent of several modern hybrids. Fragrant white flowers open from late spring and continue intermittently into autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in gritty, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Grow this compact hybrid in sharply drained, gritty soil and give it a sunny, raised position. It makes a low, dense mound well suited to rock gardens and troughs. Fragrant bright rose-pink flowers cover the plant heavily in late spring, with some later repeat.

planting.note: Plant in spring in gritty, freely draining soil in full sun.

directions: Set this compact hybrid in sharply drained, humus-rich soil in a sunny spot. It forms a neat, rounded shrub that flowers profusely for its size. Intensely fragrant deep-pink flowers open in late spring, often with a lighter second flush.

planting.note: Plant in spring in well-drained soil in full sun to part shade.

directions: Plant this deciduous Chinese species in freely draining soil in a sunny position. It is unusual among garden daphnes for its yellow flowers. Fragrant golden-yellow blooms open in late spring, followed by red berries.

planting.note: Plant in spring in gritty, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Give this Mediterranean evergreen sharply drained soil and a warm, sunny position sheltered from cold. It forms a compact, softly hairy-leaved mound. Clusters of fragrant rose-pink flowers open in late spring, sometimes repeating lightly.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a trough or rock garden with sharp drainage.

directions: Grow this dwarf hybrid in a very gritty, freely draining mix in full sun, keeping it dry in winter. It makes a tight, slow cushion ideal for troughs and raised beds. Fragrant pink flowers appear in late spring above the compact evergreen mound.

planting.note: Plant in spring in gritty, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Set this dwarf alpine hybrid in a sharply drained, gritty mix and raise it above winter wet. It forms a compact, spreading mat well suited to troughs. Fragrant deep-pink flowers cover the plant in late spring with scattered later bloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring in dry, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Plant this drought-tolerant Mediterranean evergreen in dry, freely draining soil in full sun. It suits hot, lean sites where many shrubs struggle. Fragrant creamy-white flowers open in summer, followed by red berries that are toxic.

planting.note: Plant in spring in well-drained soil in sun to part shade.

directions: Plant this evergreen Chinese species in fertile, freely draining soil in a sheltered spot. It has a long flowering season and slender, willowy foliage. Fragrant white flowers open over many weeks in summer, followed by red fruit.

planting.note: Best planted in cool, shaded ground in autumn or spring.

directions: Choose a sheltered, shaded spot with moist, fertile soil and plant at the depth the shrub grew in its pot. Water regularly the first two seasons and top-dress with leaf mould each autumn. Slender white winter flowers carry a sweet fragrance and give way to blue-black fruit.

planting.note: Plant in shade in autumn or early spring; it spreads slowly by suckers.

directions: Site in moist, well-drained soil rich in organic matter, allowing room for its suckering, thicket-forming habit. Keep evenly watered while establishing and lift stray suckers if it outgrows its space. Narrow leaves set off pink-tinged white flowers in winter, followed by black berries.

planting.note: Plant in shaded, moist ground in autumn or spring to enjoy the dark stems.

directions: Plant in fertile, humus-rich soil in shade, positioning it where the near-black young stems and pink flower buds can be seen up close. Mulch annually and water in dry spells during establishment. Fragrant pink-flushed white flowers open in late winter, followed by glossy black fruit.

planting.note: Plant in groups in shade as a low, spreading groundcover.

directions: Set plants 18 to 30 inches apart in shaded, moisture-retentive soil so they knit into a low carpet by suckering. Water through the first summers and mulch to keep roots cool. Short spikes of scented white winter flowers are followed by blue-black berries on this compact, hardiest form.

planting.note: Plant in shade or dappled light in autumn or spring.

directions: Plant in rich, well-drained soil in a shaded, sheltered position and keep the root zone mulched. Water regularly until established, after which it tolerates dry shade reasonably well. Deeply fragrant white flowers appear in winter and are followed by red berries that ripen toward dark red.

planting.note: Plant in shade where its narrow leaves and open habit can be admired.

directions: Plant in humus-rich, moist but well-drained soil in shade or part shade, giving this more refined selection a little shelter from cold winds. Mulch and water while establishing. Slim, wavy dark leaves and very fragrant white winter flowers are followed by dark berries on this Roy Lancaster introduction.

planting.note: Plant in shaded, fertile ground in autumn or spring.

directions: Plant in moist, organic-rich soil in shade, keeping it evenly watered in its early years. An annual leaf-mould mulch keeps it thriving in dry, root-filled shade. This upright species carries strongly scented white flowers early in the year, followed by shining black berries.

planting.note: Plant in sheltered shade after hard frosts pass in cold areas.

directions: Give this larger, long-leaved species moist, fertile soil in a shaded, wind-sheltered spot, as it is less cold-hardy than most sweet boxes. Water well while establishing and mulch generously. Greenish-white winter flowers are only lightly scented and are followed by purple to black fruit.

planting.note: Plant in shaded, humus-rich soil in autumn or spring.

directions: Plant in cool, moist, organic soil in shade, allowing space for its arching, broad-leaved growth. Keep watered during establishment and mulch each year. Fragrant white flowers open in winter along the stems and are followed by dark berries.

planting.note: Plant in shade as a low, uniform groundcover or edging.

directions: Space plants evenly in moist, well-drained shade so this tidy, low-mounding selection forms a dense mat. Water through the first summers and mulch to conserve moisture. Sweetly scented white flowers appear in late winter, followed by black berries on this compact groundcover form.

planting.note: Plant in shade as a rounded low hedge or foundation shrub.

directions: Plant in rich, moist but well-drained soil in shade or part shade, spacing plants for a neat, mounded low hedge. Mulch and water regularly while young. This uniform selection bears very fragrant white winter flowers on arching stems, followed by dark berries.

planting.note: Plant in shaded ground in autumn or spring for a compact evergreen.

directions: Plant in humus-rich, well-drained soil in shade, setting the crown level with the soil surface. Water through establishment and mulch each autumn to keep roots cool and moist. Bronze-tinted new growth matures to glossy green, and fragrant white flowers in late winter give way to black berries.

planting.note: Set out container plants in spring or early fall so roots establish before hard weather.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic soil with the top of the rootball level with the surrounding grade, then mulch to keep roots cool. Water deeply through the first two summers and shear lightly after the main flush to keep growth dense. Expect the tiny cream flowers to open in autumn and perfume the whole garden with an apricot-like scent.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in a sheltered, sunny spot for the heaviest flowering.

directions: Dig a hole twice as wide as the pot and backfill with loosened native soil, keeping the crown at grade. This selection flowers young and often, so a light feeding in spring supports repeated blooming. Look for dense clusters of white flowers that begin in autumn and reappear during mild spells through winter.

planting.note: Establish in spring or fall where its autumn scent can be enjoyed near a path or window.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained soil and stake young specimens against wind their first season. Prune only to shape after the flowers fade, since blooms form on maturing wood. This form carries vivid orange flowers in fall whose fragrance is even stronger than the cream-flowered type.

planting.note: Site in full sun with afternoon shelter in hotter zones and plant in spring or fall.

directions: Set the rootball at grade in loose, well-drained soil and water it in thoroughly. Give it a slow-release feeding each spring and shear lightly to encourage bushiness. The warm apricot-orange flowers appear in fall and carry a rich, fruity perfume.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall; tolerates more cold than sweet olive and makes a fine evergreen hedge.

directions: Plant in average, well-drained soil at the same depth it grew in the pot, spacing shrubs closely for a hedge. The spiny, holly-like foliage responds well to shearing, which is best done in early summer. Small white flowers hide among the leaves in autumn and release a sweet fragrance despite their size.

planting.note: Give it some afternoon shade in hot climates to protect the variegation; plant spring or fall.

directions: Plant at grade in well-drained soil and mulch to steady soil moisture. Pinch stray shoots to keep the mound tidy and remove any all-green reverting branches promptly. Grown mainly for its speckled cream-and-green new growth flushed pink, though modest white flowers still scent the air in fall.

planting.note: One of the hardier, denser selections; plant spring or fall as a screen or clipped hedge.

directions: Set plants in well-drained soil at the depth of the container and water regularly until established. Its naturally compact, upright habit needs little pruning beyond light shaping after bloom. The lustrous spiny leaves make a tight barrier, and fragrant white flowers open in autumn.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall in partial shade to keep the white leaf margins from scorching.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil at grade and mulch to conserve moisture. Prune out any solid-green shoots to preserve the crisp creamy-white leaf edges. It brightens shady corners year-round and adds discreet, sweetly scented white flowers in fall.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall as a distinctive specimen where its jagged foliage can be admired.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained soil and keep evenly moist through establishment. Its deeply cut, fiercely spined leaves are the main attraction, so prune sparingly to keep the natural form. Fragrant white flowers open in autumn among the striking foliage.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall in full sun for the deepest purple coloring on new growth.

directions: Plant at grade in well-drained soil and water in well. New shoots emerge a rich blackish-purple before maturing to dark green, so light spring pruning encourages more of the colorful flush. Sweetly fragrant white flowers appear in fall.

planting.note: A slow, compact grower; plant spring or fall for a low evergreen accent.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil at the depth of the pot and mulch lightly. Its small, rounded, cupped leaves and dense dwarf habit need almost no pruning. Tiny fragrant white flowers open in fall on this slow, tidy shrub.

planting.note: This vigorous hybrid makes a large screen; plant spring or fall with room to spread.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil at grade and water deeply while it establishes. A cross between sweet olive and holly tea olive, it combines strong fragrance with dense, spiny evergreen foliage. Prune after the fall flush of small white flowers to maintain a hedge or screen.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall; noted for cream-to-orange autumn flowers with strong scent.

directions: Set the rootball level with the soil and mulch to hold moisture. This selection produces an abundance of fragrant flowers that shade from cream to soft orange. Trim after flowering to keep it shapely as a large hedge or specimen.

planting.note: A cold-tolerant, broad-leaved selection; plant spring or fall as a dense screen.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil at grade and water through the first seasons. Its rounded leaves and rugged constitution make it one of the hardier tea olives for a full evergreen barrier. Small fragrant white flowers open in fall and can repeat in spring.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall; this spring-blooming hybrid is hardier than sweet olive.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained soil at the depth of the container and mulch. Unlike fall-blooming relatives, this dense rounded shrub covers itself in fragrant white flowers in mid-spring. Shear right after flowering if you want to hold it as a formal hedge.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall in a sheltered spot; blooms in early spring on arching stems.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil at grade, ideally with shelter from cold wind. Its small dark leaves and graceful arching branches carry masses of tubular white flowers in early spring. Prune lightly after bloom, as flowers form on the previous year's growth.

planting.note: This native shrub tolerates wet and coastal sites; plant spring or fall.

directions: Plant in moist to average, well-drained soil, though it also copes with damp ground. Native to the southeastern United States, it bears fragrant creamy flowers in spring followed by dark blue-black fruit. Little pruning is needed beyond shaping this large shrub or small tree.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall; the stiff, toothed leaves make a formidable barrier.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil at grade and water while establishing. Its large, rigid, sharply toothed leaves form a dense, uninviting hedge for boundaries. Fragrant creamy-white flowers open in autumn and may be followed by violet fruit.

planting.note: A hardy, broad, spring-flowering species; plant spring or fall in well-drained soil.

directions: Plant at grade in well-drained soil and mulch to conserve moisture. This rounded, spreading shrub has smooth, leathery green leaves rather than spines. Small fragrant white flowers cluster along the stems in spring, followed by dark fruit.

planting.note: A large, tree-like species for mild gardens; plant spring or fall with ample space.

directions: Plant in deep, well-drained soil where it has room to grow into a small tree. The long, sometimes wavy-edged leaves give it a bold texture in a woodland setting. Fragrant pale flowers open in late winter to early spring.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall in a sheltered position; flowers appear in early spring.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil at the depth of the container and shelter from cold wind. Its glossy leaves may be smooth-edged or finely toothed on the same plant. Clusters of fragrant white flowers open in early spring among the evergreen foliage.

planting.note: A compact, small-leaved form; plant spring or fall for a fine-textured hedge.

directions: Plant at grade in well-drained soil and water through establishment. Its small, mostly spineless leaves give a neat, fine texture that shears cleanly. Little white flowers scent the garden in fall despite their size.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall; give some shade in hot climates to protect the gold foliage.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil at grade and mulch to keep roots cool. The new growth emerges bright golden-yellow, aging to yellow-green, and lights up a partly shaded corner. Discreet fragrant white flowers open in autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall as a narrow vertical accent.

directions: Site this columnar selection in well-drained soil with shelter from harsh winter wind, which can splay the narrow crown. Little pruning is needed beyond removing stray shoots to keep the tight upright form. Inconspicuous spring flowers give way to occasional black fruit on this evergreen accent shrub.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall as a low mounded evergreen.

directions: Plant this dwarf, spreading cultivar in acidic, well-drained soil and mulch to keep the shallow roots cool. Its dense low mound needs only occasional shaping and makes a fine boxwood substitute. Small spring flowers are barely noticeable on the finely textured evergreen foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall for hedging or foundation use.

directions: Give this vigorous, cup-leaved holly well-drained soil and space to spread into a broad dense form. Shear as needed to maintain a hedge or let it grow naturally for a billowing shape. Female plants carry small black berries after the inconspicuous spring bloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, acidic soil; tolerates wet sites.

directions: Set this native evergreen in consistently moist, acidic soil where it can sucker into a colony over time. Prune in early spring to renew leggy stems and keep the base full. Greenish flowers in late spring bring pollinators, and female plants ripen black berries that persist into winter.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist acidic soil for a compact hedge.

directions: This compact selection holds its lower leaves better than the species, so plant it where a fuller base matters. Give it moist, acidic ground and clip lightly in spring to shape. Small spring flowers precede black fruit on female plants of this tidy native evergreen.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a boxwood alternative for edging.

directions: Plant this dwarf globe-shaped inkberry in moist acidic soil where a low evergreen ball is wanted. Its dense foliage and full base need very little pruning to stay neat. New growth emerges with a reddish tint, and tiny spring flowers are followed by small dark fruit.

planting.note: Plant in spring with a male such as 'Southern Gentleman' nearby.

directions: This heavy-fruiting female needs a late-flowering male pollinator planted within range to color up. Give it moist acidic soil and full sun for the best berry display. Bright red fruit smothers the bare stems from fall well into winter, prized for cutting.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a dwarf that still needs a male pollinator.

directions: Choose this compact female for smaller gardens and set a male winterberry nearby for pollination. Plant in moist, acidic soil and prune only lightly in late winter. It bears unusually large red berries for its size, holding them on bare branches into the cold months.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a male pollinator for late-blooming females.

directions: Plant this male selection to pollinate late-flowering females such as 'Winter Red' so they can fruit. It bears no berries itself but supplies essential pollen in late spring. Give it the same moist, acidic soil as the females and place it within roughly fifty feet of them.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall for foundations and low hedges.

directions: This compact form of Burford holly suits foundation beds and clipped hedges where the full-size plant is too large. Plant in well-drained soil and shear after the spring flush to keep it dense. It self-pollinates to bear red berries and shrugs off summer heat.

planting.note: Plant in spring with 'Blue Prince' nearby for berries.

directions: This cold-hardy blue holly needs a male such as 'Blue Prince' within range to set fruit. Plant in moist, well-drained acidic soil and shelter from drying winter wind. Glossy blue-green evergreen leaves back heavy crops of red berries that brighten the winter garden.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a male pollinator for blue hollies.

directions: Plant this male blue holly to pollinate females like 'Blue Princess' so they can fruit. It carries no berries but supplies pollen and offers the same glossy dark evergreen foliage. Give it moist, well-drained acidic soil and protection from harsh winter wind.

planting.note: Plant in spring with a blue male pollinator nearby.

directions: This vigorous female blue holly makes a fast, cold-hardy evergreen hedge when paired with a male pollinator. Plant in moist, well-drained acidic soil and clip to shape in spring. Bright red berries follow the spring bloom and persist against the dark blue-green leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring with 'China Boy' for pollination.

directions: Plant this rounded female holly with 'China Boy' nearby so it can bear fruit. It tolerates more heat than many blue hollies and keeps its glossy green foliage year-round. Give it well-drained soil, and enjoy the abundant red berries that ripen in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a male pollinator for 'China Girl'.

directions: Use this male holly to pollinate 'China Girl' and other compatible females so they fruit. It bears no berries but tolerates heat well and offers dense green evergreen foliage. Plant in well-drained soil and shear as needed for a rounded form or hedge.

planting.note: Plant in spring with 'Blue Boy' as pollinator.

directions: This hardy female blue holly forms a rounded evergreen mound and fruits when paired with a male. Plant in moist, well-drained acidic soil and protect from cold, drying wind. Red berries set after the spring flowers and stand out against the blue-green leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall as an upright evergreen screen.

directions: Plant this narrow, pyramidal holly for screening or as a specimen where a tall evergreen is wanted. Its slender dark leaves and heavy fruit set make it a Southern favorite. Give it moist, acidic, well-drained soil; red berries ripen in fall and last through winter without a separate male.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist well-drained soil; needs a male.

directions: This is the glossy, spiny-leaved holly of holiday tradition, needing a male plant nearby for berry set. Plant in moist, well-drained soil with shelter from harsh wind and prune in winter for cut greens. Female plants ripen bright red berries in fall that persist through the cold season.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall as a variegated evergreen specimen.

directions: This variegated English holly edges each spiny leaf in creamy white for year-round contrast. Plant in moist, well-drained soil and pair a male holly nearby if you want the female to fruit. Prune lightly in winter to shape and harvest festive variegated cuttings.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall as a gold-edged evergreen specimen.

directions: Despite the name, this broad-leaved holly is female and bears red berries, its nearly spineless leaves boldly margined in gold. Plant in moist, well-drained soil with a male holly nearby for fruit. Prune in late winter to shape and to gather colorful evergreen cuttings.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall as a narrow upright evergreen.

directions: This narrow, densely spiny holly makes a striking columnar accent or tight screen in small spaces. Plant in well-drained soil and provide a compatible male pollinator for berry set. Glossy dark evergreen foliage backs bright red fruit that colors up in fall on female plants.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist well-drained soil.

directions: This slow-growing evergreen carries small, boldly spined leaves closely set along stiff branches. Plant in moist, well-drained, acidic soil and give it a sheltered spot. Female plants set bright red berries tucked tight against the stems, ripening in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall as a pyramidal evergreen screen.

directions: Sold as Oakland, this pyramidal hybrid holly holds a naturally neat shape with little pruning. The glossy layered leaves emerge with a reddish cast and mature deep green. Plant in well-drained soil; it sets red berries and takes heat better than the classic blue hollies.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall as a compact pyramidal evergreen.

directions: Sold as Red Beauty, this compact pyramidal holly fits smaller yards where a tall screen is too big. Plant in well-drained soil with a male pollinator such as a blue holly nearby. Dark, spiny evergreen leaves set off heavy crops of bright red berries in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall as a low mounded evergreen.

directions: This dwarf holly has soft, flexible new growth that gives it a gentler feel than most. Plant in acidic, well-drained soil and mulch to keep the roots cool and evenly moist. Its tidy rounded mound needs little pruning and works well as a low edging or boxwood substitute.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall for low hedges and foundations.

directions: This spreading holly grows faster and with larger cupped leaves than Heller's, filling in broad low spaces quickly. Plant in acidic, well-drained soil and shear to keep it dense. Small spring flowers are barely visible amid the glossy evergreen foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring as an upright male pollinator and screen.

directions: Sold as Castle Wall, this upright male blue holly serves both as a narrow evergreen hedge and as a pollinator for Castle Spire and other females. Plant in moist, well-drained acidic soil and shear lightly to keep the columnar form. It bears no berries but supplies the pollen females need.

planting.note: Plant in spring with Castle Wall nearby for berries.

directions: Sold as Castle Spire, this narrow female blue holly makes a tidy vertical accent and fruits when paired with Castle Wall. Plant in moist, well-drained acidic soil sheltered from winter wind. Glossy dark blue-green foliage backs bright red berries that hold through winter.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist acidic soil with a male pollinator.

directions: Sold as Berry Heavy, this female winterberry lives up to its name with dense clusters of red fruit. Plant in moist to wet acidic soil and set a male such as 'Southern Gentleman' nearby. The berries cover the bare deciduous stems from fall into winter for a bold display.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a dwarf female needing a male pollinator.

directions: Sold as Berry Poppins, this compact winterberry suits small gardens and containers when paired with a male such as Mr. Poppins. Plant in moist, acidic soil in full sun for the heaviest fruit set. Bright red berries crowd the bare branches from autumn into winter.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a compact male pollinator.

directions: Sold as Mr. Poppins, this dwarf male winterberry pollinates Berry Poppins and other compatible females. It bears no fruit itself but supplies the pollen they need to color up. Plant in moist, acidic soil in full sun within range of the females it serves.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall for golden foliage color.

directions: This holly lights up with golden-yellow new growth, brightest on the sun-facing outer stems. Plant in acidic, well-drained soil and give it good light to keep the gold color strong. Shear lightly to encourage fresh colorful growth on this compact evergreen.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist well-drained soil.

directions: This bold holly bears large, leathery, nearly spineless leaves that resemble a Southern magnolia. Plant in moist, well-drained, acidic soil with some afternoon shade in hot climates. Female plants produce dense clusters of red-orange berries in fall when a male is nearby.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall as a large pyramidal screen.

directions: This hybrid forms a dense broad pyramid of large, lustrous, spiny dark-green leaves. Plant in moist, well-drained soil and provide a male such as 'James Swan' for heavy fruit. Female plants set abundant red berries in fall that persist well into winter.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall as an upright pyramidal evergreen.

directions: This upright, pyramidal Japanese holly makes a fine narrow screen or formal evergreen accent. Plant in acidic, well-drained soil and shear to keep the dense conical shape crisp. Its small dark leaves stay handsome year-round with only inconspicuous spring flowers.

planting.note: Plant in dappled shade where the winter buds can be enjoyed at close range.

directions: Site this male, non-berrying form in moist, acidic soil enriched with leaf mold and shelter it from harsh midday sun. Little pruning is needed beyond removing any wayward shoots after flowering. Deep red flower buds form in late summer and stay showy all winter before opening into fragrant white blooms in spring; use it as a pollinator for berrying females.

planting.note: Plant near a male skimmia so this female form fruits heavily.

directions: Grow in cool, moisture-retentive acidic soil in partial to full shade, mulching each spring with composted bark. Keep a male cultivar such as 'Rubella' within pollinating distance for a good berry set. This free-fruiting female bears unusually large, long-lasting scarlet berries after its spring flowers fade.

planting.note: A self-fertile form that fruits without a separate pollinator.

directions: Plant this compact, self-fertile subspecies in acidic, well-drained but moist soil in shade; alkaline soils cause yellowing leaves. Because it carries both flower types it sets its own crimson, slightly oval berries without needing a mate. Fragrant white spring flowers are followed by berries that persist through the cold months.

planting.note: Grown chiefly as a scented male pollinator for berrying forms.

directions: Give this male cultivar a shaded spot with humus-rich, acidic soil and steady moisture. Prune only lightly to shape after the flowers finish. Dense panicles of white spring flowers carry a strong lily-of-the-valley fragrance, and the shrub serves as an excellent pollen source for nearby females.

planting.note: A vigorous male hybrid valued for scented greenish flowers.

directions: Plant in moist, acidic to neutral soil in partial or full shade, allowing room for its broad, spreading habit. This male hybrid needs no pollinator and needs little pruning. Domed clusters of fragrant creamy-green flowers open in early spring above dense evergreen foliage, and it works well as a pollen source for berrying skimmias.

planting.note: A robust female form, also sold as 'Foremanii'.

directions: Grow this large female cultivar in cool, moisture-retentive acidic soil under light shade and mulch generously. Plant a male skimmia nearby to ensure heavy fruiting. Its broad leaves and abundant large red berries make it one of the showier winter-fruiting forms.

planting.note: A very compact selection for small spaces and containers.

directions: Plant this slow, low-mounding form in a shady bed or pot filled with ericaceous compost and keep it evenly moist. It comes in male and female clones, so pair them if berries are wanted. Compact clusters of scented spring flowers suit the front of a shaded border or a winter container.

planting.note: A compact variegated male grown for foliage and winter buds.

directions: Set this small male cultivar in dappled shade with acidic, free-draining but moisture-holding soil. The cream-edged green leaves take on pink and red tones in cold weather, complementing the reddish winter flower buds. Popular for winter pots and shaded borders where it needs no companion to look good.

planting.note: A female form selected for reliable heavy berrying.

directions: Plant in shade with cool, humus-rich acidic soil and keep the roots mulched through summer. Grow it alongside a male skimmia for the best fruit set. This female produces generous clusters of bright red berries that ripen in autumn and last through winter.

planting.note: A heavily fruiting female, also sold as 'Pabella'.

directions: Grow this compact female in moisture-retentive acidic soil in partial to full shade, mulching to keep the roots cool. A nearby male ensures the shrub fruits well. It is prized for dense trusses of large, long-lasting red berries and dark red winter buds.

planting.note: A dwarf male suited to troughs and small shaded corners.

directions: Plant this diminutive male form in ericaceous compost in shade and keep it steadily moist without waterlogging. Little or no pruning is required. Compact heads of fragrant spring flowers and neat evergreen foliage make it a good pollinator and a tidy container plant.

planting.note: A female form bearing unusual white berries.

directions: Give this white-fruited female shaded conditions and cool, acidic, moist soil rich in organic matter. Site it with a compatible male for reliable fruiting. Fragrant spring flowers are followed by clusters of creamy-white berries that stand out against the dark evergreen leaves.

planting.note: A low, spreading Himalayan species with aromatic leaves.

directions: Plant this suckering, spreading species in cool, moist, acidic soil in shade where its aromatic foliage releases scent when brushed. It forms low thickets over time and needs little care once established. Yellow-green spring flowers give way to red berries on female plants.

planting.note: An aromatic-leaved species from Asian mountain forests.

directions: Grow this woodland species in deep shade with humus-rich, consistently moist acidic soil. Its glossy leaves are strongly aromatic when crushed. Fragrant greenish-yellow spring flowers are followed by red berries on female plants, best set with a nearby male.

planting.note: The classic yellow-speckled female spotted laurel.

directions: Grow this female form in shade to light shade in ordinary soil; the gold-flecked leaves brighten dark corners without scorching. Plant a male aucuba nearby so it produces its glossy red berries. Prune in spring to shape and to remove any all-green reverting shoots.

planting.note: A boldly gold-speckled male form for bright foliage in shade.

directions: Plant this vigorous male cultivar in partial to full shade in fertile, moist but well-drained soil. Its large leaves are heavily splashed with yellow, lighting up shady spots. As a male it bears no berries but makes an excellent pollinator for female spotted laurels; prune in spring to keep it bushy.

planting.note: A compact, self-fertile green-leaved form that berries on its own.

directions: Plant this dwarf, self-fertile cultivar in shade in ordinary garden soil; it stays neat and rounded without pruning. Because both flower types occur on the same plant, it sets red berries without a separate pollinator. Its dark, unspotted glossy leaves and reliable fruiting make it ideal for small shaded gardens and containers.

planting.note: A striking form with a central gold blotch on each leaf.

directions: Give this cultivar light or partial shade in fertile, moist soil to keep the bold central gold splash bright. Too much deep shade dulls the coloring while full sun scorches the leaves. Prune out any wholly green reverting shoots in spring to preserve the variegation.

planting.note: A gold-spotted male valued as a pollinator and foliage plant.

directions: Plant this male cultivar in shade to partial shade in ordinary, moisture-retentive soil. Its large leaves are generously mottled with yellow, brightening dim corners. Grow it near berrying female aucubas to fertilize them, and prune in spring to maintain a dense, shapely form.

planting.note: A finely gold-speckled form giving a marbled look.

directions: Grow in partial to full shade in fertile, well-drained but moisture-holding soil. The leaves are finely dusted with yellow flecks that create a marbled effect in shaded plantings. Remove any plain green shoots as they appear and prune in spring to keep growth compact.

planting.note: A female form with yellow-margined leaves.

directions: Plant in light to partial shade in moist, fertile soil where the broad yellow leaf margins show best. Site a male aucuba close by so this female sets its red berries. Prune in spring to shape and cut out any reverted green-leaved stems.

planting.note: A green, narrow-leaved female form; also grown as 'Salicifolia'.

directions: Plant this narrow-leaved female in shade in ordinary moist soil; the slender, glossy green foliage gives a lighter, more graceful look than the spotted forms. Grow a male aucuba nearby to ensure a good crop of red berries. It tolerates dry shade and pruning once established.

planting.note: A heavily gold-speckled male form for bright foliage.

directions: Set this male cultivar in shade to partial shade in fertile, moist but well-drained soil. Its large leaves are boldly speckled and splashed with gold, giving a bright effect in dark borders. As a male it produces no berries but pollinates nearby females; prune in spring to keep it full.

planting.note: A green female form with distinctly toothed leaf edges.

directions: Grow this female cultivar in shade in ordinary moist soil; its glossy green leaves are edged with coarse teeth. Plant it with a male aucuba for reliable red berries. It handles dry shade and responds well to spring pruning.

planting.note: A compact, round-leaved green form for smaller gardens.

directions: Plant this dwarf, round-leaved cultivar in shade in moist, fertile soil where its neat habit suits smaller borders and pots. The plain green foliage is dense and glossy. Grow a male nearby for berries on female plants, and prune lightly in spring only if needed.

planting.note: A narrow-leaved species from the Himalayas for shaded gardens.

directions: Plant this species in partial to deep shade in moist, humus-rich soil; its narrow, tapering green leaves give it a more refined look than Japanese aucuba. Pair male and female plants to obtain the red winter berries. It tolerates dry shade and can be pruned in spring to control size.

planting.note: Plant in late spring in a shaded, wind-protected border or large container.

directions: Set out in fertile, moisture-retentive soil where the cream-edged leaves are shielded from harsh midday sun that can scorch the pale margins. Water regularly in dry spells and shelter from cold winds, since variegated forms are slightly more tender than the plain species. Small white autumn flower globes appear on established plants.

planting.note: Plant in spring in deep shade to preserve the frosted white speckling.

directions: Grow in moist, humus-rich soil in a shaded spot, as deep shade intensifies the fine white stippling that gives each leaf a cobweb look. Keep soil consistently damp and feed lightly in spring for lush foliage. Established plants throw creamy flower clusters in autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sheltered shady bed or patio pot.

directions: Plant in rich, well-drained soil in shade so the soft grey-green and cream marbling stays crisp. Water steadily during warm weather and protect from freezing wind in the coldest zones. Rounded white flower heads follow in fall on mature specimens.

planting.note: Plant in spring where dappled light brings out the yellow leaf splashes.

directions: Set into moist, fertile soil in partial shade, since a little filtered light deepens the buttery gold variegation without scorching. Keep evenly watered and shelter from cold wind. Creamy autumn flower balls appear on settled plants.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a shaded, sheltered position.

directions: Grow in damp, humus-rich soil in shade to display the irregular gold and green cloud-like markings. Maintain steady moisture and mulch the roots each spring. Small white flower umbels open in autumn on established plants.

planting.note: Plant in spring in light shade to bring out the yellow leaf tips.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile soil in partial shade so the golden-yellow leaf margins color well. Water regularly and avoid harsh afternoon sun on the foliage. Rounded creamy flower clusters appear in fall on mature plants.

planting.note: Plant in spring; the dense compact habit suits smaller shaded beds.

directions: Set out in rich, moist, well-drained soil in shade, where its stocky, low-branching form fills space without growing tall. Keep the roots cool and mulched and prune lightly to shape. Creamy-white autumn flower heads appear on established shrubs.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade to keep the fine white speckling bright.

directions: Grow in moist, humus-rich soil in shade, where the tie-dye-like white flecking across the leaves shows to best effect. Water consistently and shelter from cold drying wind. White flower globes follow in autumn on settled plants.

planting.note: Plant in late spring in a warm, sheltered, lightly shaded spot.

directions: Plant in deep, fertile, moisture-retentive soil in partial shade, giving room for its large, deeply cut palmate leaves. Keep well watered in summer and protect from hard frost in colder gardens. Airy white flower panicles appear in autumn, followed by dark berries.

planting.note: Plant in late spring in a sheltered, part-shaded bed with room to spread.

directions: Set into rich, moist soil in partial shade where the narrow, finger-like leaf lobes create a bold tropical texture. Water freely in warm weather and mulch to keep roots cool. Loose sprays of white flowers open in autumn on mature specimens.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade; stake the lax stems or let them lean on a wall.

directions: Plant this sterile ivy-Fatsia hybrid in moist, fertile soil in shade, tying the sprawling stems to a support or pinching tips for a bushier shape. Keep evenly watered and shelter from cold wind. Pale green flower clusters may open in autumn but rarely set fruit.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade to protect the white leaf edges from scorch.

directions: Grow in moist, well-drained soil in shade, where the creamy-white leaf margins stay clean and bright. Support the trailing stems and keep the roots cool and damp. Insignificant greenish autumn flowers may appear on older plants.

planting.note: Plant in spring in light shade to keep the gold central blotch vivid.

directions: Set into fertile, moist soil in dappled shade so the bright yellow-green leaf centers glow. Tie the lax stems to a frame and water regularly through the growing season. This vigorous form is grown for foliage rather than its minor autumn flowers.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade; the wavy-edged leaves suit containers and shaded borders.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich soil in shade, giving support to the leaning stems as they lengthen. Its glossy leaves have distinctly ruffled margins, so keep the plant sheltered and evenly watered for the fullest form. Grown chiefly for foliage; flowers are sparse and greenish.

planting.note: Plant in spring in light shade to keep the bold cream margins bright.

directions: Grow in fertile, moist, well-drained soil in partial shade, where the wide creamy-white leaf edges show strongly. Support or lean the stems and keep the soil consistently damp. This Japanese selection is prized for its variegated foliage rather than its minor flowers.

planting.note: Plant this low, spreading form in spring for groundcover-scale coverage.

directions: Space plants close for a low massed planting and water regularly until roots reach into surrounding soil. New spring growth flushes bronze before turning glossy green, and the foliage picks up burgundy tints in cold weather. Yellow spring flowers give way to blue-black berries that birds strip quickly.

planting.note: Set out in spring in dappled shade with room to fill a low mound.

directions: Plant in humus-rich soil and keep evenly moist during the first season. This selection is prized for unusually heavy trusses of deep-yellow spring flowers on a compact frame. Expect abundant blue berries by summer and reddish winter foliage on exposed shoots.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; the name means emerald, for its glossy foliage.

directions: Site in partial shade with steady moisture until established, then it withstands short dry spells. The emerald-green new leaves and bronze young shoots make this a tidy foliage shrub for shaded borders. Rich yellow flowers appear in spring ahead of clusters of blue fruit.

planting.note: Plant in spring where its bronze-orange new growth will be seen.

directions: Grow in fertile, well-drained soil and water through dry stretches while young. This selection is grown mainly for its fiery bronze-orange spring growth that deepens to red in winter cold. Small yellow flower clusters in spring precede the usual blue-black berries.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a cold-exposed spot for the strongest winter color.

directions: Set in well-drained soil with morning sun to intensify leaf color and water until established. Foliage turns rich reddish-purple through autumn and winter, a striking contrast to the yellow spring flowers. Blue-black grape-like berries follow in summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in cool, shaded woodland soil that stays lightly moist.

directions: Site in shade with rich, acidic, humusy soil and keep the root zone cool with mulch. Its long, fern-like leaves of many leaflets make a fine low understory plant beneath conifers. Upright yellow flower spikes open in spring, followed by clusters of blue berries.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in a shaded, wind-sheltered border.

directions: Set in humus-rich, well-drained soil out of drying wind and mulch the roots. Prized for arching, spreading racemes of fragrant pale-yellow flowers through winter, often lily-of-the-valley scented. Blue-purple berries follow, and old leggy stems can be cut back after flowering to force fresh growth.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall as a bold winter-flowering specimen.

directions: Plant in fertile, moisture-retentive soil in partial shade and water well the first year. This vigorous hybrid throws tall, spreading spires of scented yellow flowers from late autumn into winter. Remove faded flower spikes and shorten leggy stems in spring to keep it well furnished.

planting.note: Plant in a shaded border where its winter blooms can be enjoyed close up.

directions: Grow in rich, well-drained soil in dappled shade and keep evenly moist while establishing. It carries dense, upright clusters of fragrant bright-yellow flowers through the darkest months. Prune out the oldest stems after flowering to encourage a fuller, more branched habit.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall for tall, upright winter flower spikes.

directions: Set in humus-rich soil in partial shade and water deeply during dry spells the first season. This selection is noted for stiffly erect racemes of deep-yellow flowers in late autumn and early winter. Trim back overly tall stems in spring to maintain a balanced, leafy framework.

planting.note: Plant in a shaded spot; a more compact and denser hybrid than most.

directions: Plant in fertile, moist but well-drained soil in partial shade and mulch the roots. This is a tidier, bushier selection carrying upright spikes of fragrant yellow flowers in early winter. Berries ripen blue in spring, and light pruning after bloom keeps the plant compact.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall for arching, branched winter flower sprays.

directions: Grow in rich, well-drained soil in light shade and keep watered through the first summer. It bears long, spreading sprays of scented yellow flowers early in the winter season. Cut back the oldest canes after flowering to renew growth from the base.

planting.note: Plant in a shaded border for its two-toned winter flowers.

directions: Set in fertile, well-drained soil in partial shade and water regularly while establishing. This more compact hybrid is grown for flower spikes that open from red-flushed buds to warm yellow. It blooms through winter and stays denser than the older Media selections.

planting.note: Plant in fall or spring for early winter bloom in shaded gardens.

directions: Plant in humus-rich soil out of harsh wind and water through the establishment season. It sends up tall, erect racemes of fragrant yellow flowers earlier than many hybrids, often in late autumn. Powder-blue berries follow, and old stems can be shortened in spring to keep it shapely.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade for narrow, softly toothed foliage and autumn bloom.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained soil in partial shade and water regularly through the first year. It offers slender, gently toothed leaflets and a graceful, arching habit for shaded borders. Scented yellow flower spikes appear in autumn ahead of blue-black fruit.

planting.note: Plant in a sheltered, frost-protected shaded spot in spring.

directions: Give it rich, moist, well-drained soil in a spot protected from cold winds and hard frost. Long ladder-like leaves and a tall, upright silhouette make it a dramatic architectural evergreen. Dense clusters of yellow flowers open in late autumn and winter, followed by blue berries.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and lean, fast-draining desert soil.

directions: Set in dry, well-drained soil in full sun and water only until established, as it resents wet roots. The small, holly-like blue-gray leaflets suit hot, arid gardens and rock plantings. Pale-yellow spring flowers give way to inflated dry yellowish-red berries.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and dry, gravelly soil of the Southwest.

directions: Plant in sharply drained, alkaline soil in full sun and water minimally after establishment. Its narrow, spiny blue-gray leaflets thrive in heat, drought, and reflected desert sun. Yellow spring flowers are followed by juicy deep-red berries used for jelly and wine.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sun or light shade; tolerant of coastal conditions.

directions: Grow in well-drained soil in sun to part shade and water occasionally once the roots take hold. Its glossy, wavy holly-like leaves flush bronze and redden in cold weather. Bright-yellow flower clusters open in spring, followed by blue-black berries; it tolerates drought and coastal wind.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sun or part shade for glossy, wavy-edged foliage.

directions: Set in fertile, well-drained soil in sun to light shade and water through the first summer. This selection is grown for lustrous, strongly wavy-margined leaflets that turn bronze in winter. Deep-yellow flower clusters appear in spring, followed by blue berries.

planting.note: Plant in spring for an upright habit and bright bronze new growth.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil in sun to part shade and keep watered while establishing. It grows more upright than the species, with young growth flushing coppery-bronze before greening. Yellow flowers cluster along the stems in spring, giving way to blue-black fruit.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in shade; a sister seedling to 'Charity'.

directions: Grow in rich, moist but well-drained soil in partial shade and water through establishment. One of the original Media seedlings, it carries upright spires of scented yellow winter flowers. Prune out old stems after bloom to keep the base clothed with foliage.

planting.note: Plant in shade in spring or fall; another of the Charity sister seedlings.

directions: Set in humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade and keep watered the first year. This selection bears upright racemes of fragrant yellow flowers through the winter months. Cut back leggy canes after flowering to renew a dense, leafy shape.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and dry, well-drained soil; a rare California native.

directions: Plant in sandy, fast-draining soil in full sun and water only until established, as it is very drought tolerant. Its slender blue-gray, spiny leaflets form a graceful mounding shrub for dry gardens. Yellow spring flowers are followed by red berries that ripen to a deeper hue.

planting.note: Plant in spring for upright growth and strong purple winter color.

directions: Set in well-drained soil in sun to part shade and water regularly while establishing. This more upright selection has blue-green leaflets that turn deep purple-bronze in winter cold. Yellow flower clusters open in spring, followed by the usual blue-black grape-like berries.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sheltered shaded border for its striking two-tone leaves.

directions: Grow in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade sheltered from cold wind. Its leaflets are green above and vivid chalky-white beneath, a distinctive feature in the shade garden. Unusual red-and-yellow flowers dangle on slender stalks in late summer, followed by dark berries.

planting.note: Plant in spring where its colorful young growth will show off.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil in sun to light shade and water until established. It is grown chiefly for young foliage that emerges apricot-bronze and matures through pink and pale green. Yellow spring flowers precede blue berries, and cold weather deepens the leaf tints.

planting.note: Transplant nursery-grown shrubs in spring or fall into well-drained ground.

directions: Dig a hole twice the width of the rootball and plant with the crown flush to grade. This selection stays denser and rounder than the species and needs little pruning beyond removing stray shoots. Expect the same fiery red fall color that gives the plant its name; the corky winged twigs add winter interest.

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring or early autumn in average garden soil.

directions: Site this compact, slow-growing form where its final size of four to five feet suits a foundation or low hedge. Backfill with native soil, water in well, and mulch to conserve moisture. It rarely needs shaping and colors a deep rose-red to crimson in fall, with far fewer fruits than the species.

planting.note: Set out in spring or fall; tolerates a wide range of soils once established.

directions: Plant with the top of the rootball even with the soil surface and water regularly the first year. This rounded selection is prized for exceptionally intense red autumn foliage and good cold hardiness. Prune in late winter if needed to maintain shape; the small flowers are inconspicuous.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; it roots wherever stems touch moist soil.

directions: Space plants a couple of feet apart for groundcover or train stems up a wall where they will cling by rootlets. Water until established, then it is quite drought tolerant and adapts to sun or shade. The evergreen foliage carries the plant year-round; note it can spread aggressively and is considered invasive in some regions.

planting.note: Transplant in spring or fall into well-drained soil in sun or light shade.

directions: Plant this white-margined form at grade and mulch to keep roots cool and moist. It mounds into a dense shrub or scrambles up a support, with green leaves edged crisp white that flush pink-tinged in winter cold. Shear in spring to keep it tidy; remove any all-green reverting shoots promptly.

planting.note: Set out in spring or fall; happiest in full sun for brightest gold color.

directions: Plant at grade in well-drained soil and give it sun to bring out the vivid gold leaf margins. It forms a low, spreading mound and can climb a short wall if stems find purchase. Foliage takes on rosy tints in winter; trim in spring and pull out any green reversions to keep the variegation.

planting.note: Plant as groundcover in spring or fall, spacing plants for quick knit.

directions: Set plants a foot or two apart on slopes or beds where you want a fast evergreen carpet. Roots form where stems touch the ground, so it fills in quickly and holds soil well. The dark green leaves turn plum-purple through winter, greening again in spring; it can be invasive, so keep it away from natural areas.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; more upright and shrubby than the trailing types.

directions: Use this stiff, upright selection as a low evergreen hedge or foundation plant, spacing for the width you want. Plant at grade, water to establish, and shear as needed for a formal outline. The glossy dark green leaves hold all winter in mild zones; flowers and fruit are minor.

planting.note: Transplant in spring or fall into average, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant this compact mound at grade in sun or part shade and mulch lightly. Each leaf shows a bright yellow-green center with a green margin, giving a glowing effect in the border. It needs minimal pruning; snip out any plain-green shoots to preserve the variegated look.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; will climb a wall or fence if given support.

directions: Grow as a shrubby mound or let it clamber up a shaded wall where it clings on its own. Plant at grade, water well, and provide light shade to protect the pale margins from scorch. New spring growth opens creamy yellow before settling to green edged in white; trim to shape after the flush.

planting.note: Plant closely for a fine-textured evergreen mat in spring or fall.

directions: Tuck this miniature form into rock gardens or use it as a tight groundcover, setting plants a foot apart. The tiny dark leaves creep flat over soil and stone, rooting as they go. Keep it evenly moist while establishing; it seldom needs pruning and rarely flowers.

planting.note: Set out in spring or fall in sun to light shade.

directions: Plant this upright, bushy form at grade and water through the first season. Leaves are green with a broad bright yellow margin that lights up a foundation planting. Prune lightly in spring for density and remove any reverting green stems.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; give sun for the strongest gold coloring.

directions: Plant this compact selection at grade and mulch to conserve moisture. Each leaf carries a large butter-yellow center framed by a thin green edge, making a bright accent in small spaces. Shear lightly to shape and pull any all-green shoots to keep the bold variegation.

planting.note: Transplant in spring or fall into moist, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant this dwarf, mounding form where its speckled foliage can be admired up close. New growth emerges heavily mottled white before maturing to green flecked with cream. Keep evenly watered while it settles; trim in spring and remove greener stems that overtake the variegation.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun to part shade.

directions: Use this low, spreading form as an evergreen groundcover or edger, planting at grade. Dark green leaves each bear a bold gold splash in the center for a two-toned effect. Water to establish, then it needs little care beyond removing reverting green shoots.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; grow as a mounded shrub or climbing cover.

directions: This vigorous, large-leaved form sprawls into a shrub or climbs walls and stumps by clinging rootlets. Plant at grade and give room, as it spreads widely. It is notable for producing showy orange-seeded pink fruit capsules in fall more freely than most wintercreepers.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in mild climates; tolerates coastal exposure.

directions: Set this glossy-leaved evergreen at grade in well-drained soil; it shrugs off salt spray and makes a fine seaside hedge. Water while establishing, then it is fairly drought tolerant in sun or part shade. Clip to shape in late spring; watch for powdery mildew in humid, crowded plantings.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; give full sun for the brightest gold edges.

directions: Plant this popular hedge shrub at grade in sun to bring out the vivid gold-margined leaves. Water regularly the first year, then keep it lightly pruned for a dense screen. Remove any all-green shoots as they appear, since they grow faster and can take over the variegation.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall as a low, boxwood-like edging or hedge.

directions: Use this small-leaved dwarf as a substitute for boxwood in low formal hedges, spacing plants close for a solid line. Plant at grade and water to establish. Shear two or three times a season to keep the tight, dense outline; it responds well to frequent clipping.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil, sun to light shade.

directions: Plant this upright evergreen at grade for a bright hedge or specimen. Its glossy green leaves carry a clean creamy-white margin that holds color year-round. Water while establishing and shear to shape in late spring; pull green reversions to maintain the white edging.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; upright habit suits screens and hedges.

directions: Set this columnar, golden-variegated form at grade for a tall narrow accent or screen. Green leaves are edged in rich yellow that brightens in full sun. Water to establish and prune lightly to keep the upright shape; remove any green-reverting stems.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall for a narrow, upright evergreen column.

directions: Use this tightly upright form for vertical accents or narrow hedging, spacing plants a couple of feet apart. Plant at grade and water through the first season. The dense glossy green foliage needs only light trimming to stay columnar; tolerates coastal and urban conditions.

planting.note: Plant in fall or early spring; seed needs cold stratification to sprout.

directions: Plant this large deciduous shrub or small tree at grade in any decent soil, including chalk. Water to establish; it is otherwise low-maintenance and tolerant of a range of sites. The real show comes in autumn, when four-lobed rosy-pink capsules split to reveal orange-coated seeds against yellow-red foliage; all parts are toxic if eaten.

planting.note: Plant in fall or spring; branches arch under heavy fruit.

directions: Plant this heavy-fruiting selection at grade where its arching, berry-laden branches can be seen in autumn. It is more free-fruiting than the species, with abundant rose-pink capsules that open to orange seeds. Water to establish and prune only to shape; the fall foliage turns rich red. Fruit is toxic if eaten.

planting.note: Plant in fall or spring in moist woodland-edge conditions.

directions: This native of eastern U.S. woodlands prefers moist, humus-rich soil and dappled shade, so plant it at grade beneath high canopy. Water during dry spells to keep it happy. Its common name 'hearts-a-bustin' comes from warty crimson capsules that split open in fall to dangle scarlet seeds; browsing deer often keep it grazed.

planting.note: Plant in fall or spring in fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: Give this vigorous Asian spindle room to grow into a large shrub or small tree, planting at grade in decent soil. Water while establishing, then it is undemanding. It is grown for reliable pink-and-orange fruit capsules and often excellent pink-to-red autumn leaf color; seeds are toxic.

planting.note: Plant in fall or spring; corky-winged stems add winter interest.

directions: Plant this upright Chinese spindle at grade in any reasonable soil in sun or light shade. Its stems develop broad corky wings that stand out in winter once the leaves drop. In autumn it carries abundant four-lobed pink capsules that open to orange seeds, with foliage turning yellow to pink; fruit is toxic.

planting.note: Plant in fall or spring in moist, fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant this handsome large spindle at grade where its fall display can be enjoyed. Water to establish; it takes sun or part shade and is quite hardy. It is prized for large scarlet-red capsules that split to reveal orange seeds and for brilliant crimson autumn foliage; all parts are toxic.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; makes a fast semi-evergreen screen or hedge.

directions: Plant this vigorous glossy-leaved shrub at grade for a quick hedge or privacy screen. It is evergreen in mild winters and semi-evergreen where cold, dropping some leaves. Water to establish and shear to shape; it produces pinkish fruit and can be prone to scale insects, so monitor the stems.

planting.note: Plant in fall or spring; grows into a large shrub or small tree.

directions: Give this graceful, spreading spindle room to reach tree size, planting at grade in average soil. It is adaptable and tolerant of tough urban conditions once established. Pinkish-yellow capsules open to orange seeds in fall as the light green foliage turns soft pink; seeds are toxic if eaten.

planting.note: Plant in fall or spring in moist, well-drained woodland soil.

directions: Plant this slow-growing spindle at grade in part shade with steady moisture. It develops an elegant open form over time and needs little pruning. In autumn the leaves color deep purple-red while pendulous carmine capsules split to show orange seeds; the fruit is poisonous.

planting.note: Plant in fall or spring in fertile, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Plant this large-leaved European spindle at grade in sun or part shade with good moisture. Water while establishing and prune only to shape. It bears sizeable rose-red winged capsules that open to orange seeds, hanging on long stalks, with warm red fall color; all parts are toxic.

planting.note: Plant in fall or spring; suits rock gardens and low, spreading cover.

directions: Plant this exceptionally hardy dwarf spindle at grade where its low, arching stems can spread and layer. It has fine narrow leaves and a delicate texture well suited to rock gardens. Water to establish; small four-part pink capsules open to orange seeds in fall, and foliage flushes reddish before dropping.

planting.note: Plant in fall or spring; young stems are dotted with corky warts.

directions: Plant this slow, twiggy spindle at grade in sun or part shade in well-drained soil. Its green stems are speckled with the corky warts that give the plant its name, a subtle year-round feature. Small brownish flowers give way to pink capsules with black-and-orange seeds in fall; all parts are toxic.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; low mounding habit for edges and mass plantings.

directions: Plant this bright, low-mounding form at grade in sun to part shade and mulch to hold moisture. Green leaves carry a bold golden margin that gives a cheerful glow to foundations and borders. Water to establish, shear lightly in spring, and pull any green-reverting shoots to keep the variegation.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall for a fast upright evergreen screen.

directions: Use this vigorous upright form for tall narrow hedges and screens, spacing plants a couple of feet apart. Plant at grade and water through the first year to build a strong root system. Its dense glossy green foliage responds well to shearing; it tolerates coastal salt and urban air.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in average, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant this rounded selection at grade and water deeply the first two seasons. It was chosen for dependable, intense red fall color that develops early and holds well. Prune in late winter only as needed for shape; as with all burning bush, be mindful of its self-seeding and invasive tendency in some areas.

planting.note: Set out young plants in spring or early fall in most soils, including chalky ground.

directions: Space plants closely along a line for a formal hedge and firm the soil around the roots after planting. Cut back the top third after planting to encourage dense low branching, then shear two or three times through the growing season. Clusters of small white flowers appear in early summer if the plant is left unclipped.

planting.note: Plant in spring or autumn in any reasonable garden soil in sun or light shade.

directions: Dig a planting hole wide enough to spread the roots and set the plant at the depth it grew in the nursery. Prune lightly each year to keep a tidy outline, tolerating a wide range of soils including limey ground. Panicles of creamy-white flowers open in early to midsummer and are followed by glossy black berries.

planting.note: Give this large plant plenty of room and plant in spring or fall in deep soil.

directions: Plant as a specimen tree where its eventual size will not crowd buildings, and stake young trunks the first season. Limb up the lower branches gradually to reveal the smooth grey bark, or leave it bushy as a tall screen. Large sprays of white summer flowers ripen into heavy clusters of dark blue-black berries by autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; grows in almost any soil and self-sows freely.

directions: Set plants at nursery depth and keep them watered through the first summer. Shear regularly to control the vigorous, arching growth and deadhead to limit seedy spread where it is invasive. Loose panicles of small white flowers open in late spring and carry a strong scent.

planting.note: A very cold-tolerant privet; plant in spring or fall in ordinary soil.

directions: Plant in a hole slightly wider than the root ball and backfill firmly. Allow the horizontally tiered branches to develop for an informal hedge, or clip for a denser form. Nodding clusters of white flowers open in early summer, followed by dark berries with a bluish bloom.

planting.note: One of the hardiest privets; plant spring or fall for a clipped hedge.

directions: Space plants tightly in a row and cut the tops back hard after planting to build a full base. Shear several times a season to hold a formal shape, as it tolerates heavy pruning well. Small white flowers appear in early summer on unclipped stems and give off a heavy fragrance.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil; late bloom is its main asset.

directions: Set the plant at its original depth and give it room to arch gracefully rather than clipping it hard. Prune only to remove wayward stems, since much of its charm is the loose habit and late flush. Long, airy panicles of white flowers open in late summer, weeks after most privets have finished.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in average soil; suited to informal borders.

directions: Plant with the crown level with the surrounding soil and mulch lightly to conserve moisture. Let the graceful, spreading branches grow with only light thinning, as this species looks best untrimmed. Nodding clusters of white flowers open in early summer along the arching shoots.

planting.note: A small-leaved evergreen often used for topiary; plant in spring in a sheltered site.

directions: Plant in free-draining soil and protect from cold drying winds while young. The tiny dark leaves take clipping well, making it a favorite for clipped balls, cones, and spirals. Small white flowers appear in summer on unclipped growth, followed by small black fruit.

planting.note: An uncommon hardy privet; plant in spring or fall in ordinary soil.

directions: Plant at nursery depth and water through the first season to settle the roots. Grow it as an informal upright shrub, thinning old stems at the base rather than shearing. White flower clusters open in early summer, and the leaves can take on purplish tints in autumn.

planting.note: Prized for tiered horizontal branches; plant in spring or fall in average soil.

directions: Plant this low, wide-spreading form where its flat, layered branches can be seen from the side. Prune only to remove dead wood, since shearing spoils the distinctive tiered habit. Nodding white flower clusters line the horizontal stems in early summer, followed by blue-black berries.

planting.note: Grow in full sun for the brightest gold leaves; plant in spring or fall.

directions: Plant in a sunny spot, since shade turns the foliage a dull green rather than gold. Prune in late winter to keep the color-bearing young growth coming, cutting back about a third of the stems. Small white flowers appear in early summer against the yellow leaves, though the foliage is the main show.

planting.note: A fast, cold-tough hedge privet; plant in spring or fall in a row.

directions: Space plants closely and cut them back by a third at planting to force branching from the base. Shear two or three times a season to maintain a crisp formal hedge, as it responds vigorously to clipping. Small white flowers open in early summer on any stems left unpruned.

planting.note: A compact glossy evergreen for hedges; plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil.

directions: Plant at nursery depth and water deeply until established, then let it dry slightly between waterings. Shear into a formal hedge or clip as a small standard, pruning after the flowers fade. Compact clusters of white flowers open in late spring against the thick, waxy dark green leaves.

planting.note: A slow, stiff dwarf with rounded curled leaves; plant in spring in good soil.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil and give it a spot with some afternoon shade in hot climates. Its slow, congested growth needs little pruning, so remove only stray shoots to keep the dense form. Short spikes of white flowers appear in early summer among the crowded, cupped dark leaves.

planting.note: Narrow, wavy-edged leaves on an upright plant; plant in spring or fall.

directions: Set the plant at nursery depth and water it in well, choosing a site with good drainage. Its upright habit suits a screen or clipped hedge, and it takes shearing after bloom. Fragrant white flower clusters open in late spring above the twisted, rippled leaves.
